danger_signals.visual_form: G.I. Ant positioned above screen edge or platform edge
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent death; player must avoid dropping below safe zones

danger_signals.visual_form: Dominos toppling uncontrollably or cascading across screen
danger_signals.meaning: Level failure state; unintended domino destruction blocks puzzle solution

danger_signals.visual_form: G.I. Ant directly beneath falling domino pieces
danger_signals.meaning: Death state; collision with falling dominoes results in failure

danger_signals.visual_form: Exit door obscured by fallen dominoes, destroyed ledges, or unbridged gaps
danger_signals.meaning: Puzzle unsolvable; player cannot reach goal even if all dominoes fall

danger_signals.visual_form: Time counter reaching zero on HUD
danger_signals.meaning: Puzzle deadline exceeded; hint system activates but level can still continue

safety_signals.visual_form: Solid ledges and interconnected platforms holding dominoes
safety_signals.meaning: Safe movement space for G.I. Ant; allows navigation without falling

safety_signals.visual_form: Open doorway visible and reachable after domino chain completes
safety_signals.meaning: Level completion is possible; exit path remains clear
Player can reset to pre-push state; provides safety margin for experimentation

safety_signals.visual_form: Dominoes standing upright in intended configuration matching visual layout
safety_signals.meaning: Puzzle setup is valid; ready for single-push completion attempt

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: G.I. Ant is the controllable entity; appears as large ant figure scaled relative to dominoes

faction_indicators.faction: Colin Curly / Quavers mascot (Original branding - Amiga/Atari/DOS)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Packets retrieved after each themed world; 9 packets total as progression markers
Cash bundles dropped down ant hill; substitutes for Quavers in SNES localization

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Factory/mechanical themed level environment with industrial aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: First world; introduces basic domino mechanics and tutorial levels

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ancient Mesoamerican temple architecture and cultural visual identity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mid-game progression; increasing puzzle complexity with themed domino configurations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Futuristic sci-fi environment with technical elements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Advanced puzzle mechanics; spatial navigation challenges
Complex domino interaction patterns; puzzle interdependencies

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Classical Grecian architecture and columned environments
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High-difficulty progression; multi-stage puzzle solving required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Castle fortification architecture with feudal aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Late-game challenges; complex level layouts and domino types

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mechanical construction toy aesthetic; modular metal framework visuals
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Advanced puzzle mechanics; building/construction themed challenges

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Underground cavern and dungeon stone architecture
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Near-end game state; maximum puzzle complexity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Eastern religious architecture and Japanese cultural design elements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final world; level 100 features hidden domino markings requiring special solution methods

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Exit door opens visually when all dominoes successfully topple
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression granted; token awarded; next level unlocked

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: G.I. Ant death animation or domino destruction visual feedback
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Message displays 'You Failed, You Died'; level reset to initial state or token checkpoint

environmental_storytelling.example: Nine distinct visual environments (Industrial → Aztec → Space → Electronic → Greek → Medieval → Meccano → Dungeon → Japanese) communicate narrative journey through varied locales where dominoes must be manipulated
Quavers packets (original branding) or cash bundles (SNES) retrieved after each world create tangible progression reward and reinforce recovery/rescue objective
Each themed world's environmental visual identity (temple columns, futuristic panels, castle stones) reinforces world-state through visual design without explicit dialogue
'You Failed, You Died' message paired with visual death animation communicates consequence and narrative stakes for failed attempts
Level 98 hint revealing designer admits unsolvable state ('I can't find it!') breaks fourth wall; communicates intentional design challenge and world-building quirk

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can navigate platforms, carry dominoes one at a time, position for push actions
Descends below screen or platform edge; triggers death state and level failure
Directly beneath falling domino; collision results in immediate level failure
Stable; waiting to be pushed; identified by red and yellow pattern markings
Falls according to type-specific mechanics; initiates chain reaction; different types create different actions (bridge gaps, destroy ledges, etc.)
Landing on another domino causes destruction; registers as level failure condition
Inaccessible; blocks level completion; remains sealed if puzzle unsolved or path obstructed
Accessible after all dominoes topple; allows G.I. Ant to progress to next level
Displays remaining time; countdown toward zero triggers hint system when expired
Reaches zero; hint system activates on pause; does not force level failure (puzzle can continue)

notes: Pushover is a pure domino-chain puzzle game with strong environmental storytelling through themed worlds (9 total). Visual communication relies on: (1) domino pattern identification via red/yellow markings for type recognition; (2) spatial platform layout conveying puzzle complexity; (3) failure states (death message, falling, crushing) providing immediate feedback; (4) world themes providing narrative context without explicit exposition. The SNES version localized branding from Quavers (UK snack mascot Colin Curly) to Captain Rat/cash bundles. Level 100 introduces hidden domino markings as a final difficulty spike. The game's design philosophy emphasizes singular or limited-solution puzzles, with intentional dead-end scenarios teaching players causal domino mechanics. No faction conflict or political systems are present; all narrative is implicit progression through environmental themes.
